text:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown significant promise in automated reasoning and code generation, yet their utility in scientific research is frequently limited by "hallucinations" and an inability to produce verifiable new knowledge. This paper introduces FunSearch (searching in the function space), an evolutionary system that overcomes these limitations by pairing a pre-trained LLM with a systematic evaluator. Unlike traditional search methods that focus on finding specific solutions (the "what"), FunSearch evolves computer programs that describe "how" to solve a problem. 

This approach has led to the first verifiable scientific discoveries made using LLMs for established open problems. Specifically, FunSearch discovered new, larger constructions for the cap set problem in extremal combinatorics—the most significant improvement to the asymptotic lower bound in two decades. Furthermore, the system successfully developed novel, high-performing heuristics for the online bin packing problem, surpassing classic algorithmic benchmarks. Because the outputs are human-readable programs, they are not only concise and scalable to large instances but also highly interpretable, allowing researchers to extract new theoretical insights from the discovered code. FunSearch demonstrates that combining the creative potential of LLMs with rigorous validation can bridge the gap between AI and genuine scientific discovery.
